perturbation.original_text: Any information considered Private Information by the Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) , any personal health information as defined by the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA), and by the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) is also considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Any information considered Private Information, as determined solely by the Provider, is also considered Confidential Information. The provider is exempt from adhering to the Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) , the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA), and the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA).
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement to adhere to privacy laws (MFIPPA, PHIPA, PIPEDA) and allows the Provider to unilaterally decide what constitutes Private Information. This could lead to breaches of privacy and violations of these acts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: MFIPPA establishes rules for access to information and protection of privacy held by municipal governments. The modified text exempts the provider, directly contradicting the law's application to entities handling municipal information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (Ontario) - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act ) Ontario, Canada statute Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act Legislative Assembly of Ontario Citation Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 1 Territorial extent Province of Ontario Commenced 1 January 1988 Status: In force The Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act ( French : Loi sur l’accès à l’information et la protection de la vie privée , R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31) (commonly abbreviated FIPPA ) (the Act) is an Act of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario . The Act legislates access to information held by public institutions in Ontario subject to specific requirements to safeguard the personal information of individuals. [ 1 ] [ 2 ] History [ edit ] In 1977, the Williams Commission was convened with a mandate from Ontario's Attorney General to report on public information policies of the Government of Ontario. The Commission presented recommendations to the provincial legislature in August, 1980. [ 3 ] After the long-standing Progressive Conservative government was defeated in 1985 , the Liberal party established a minority government with the support of the New Democratic Party (NDP). One of the conditions for the NDP's support was passage of Bill 34, legislation which would establish new freedom of information and privacy protection law, and which relied on the recommendations of the Williams Commission. Bill 34 was originally introduced in July, 1985 and referred for public consultations between March 1986 and May 1987. [ 4 ] The resulting Act came into effect on January 1, 1988. [ 3 ] See also [ edit ] Freedom of information in Canada Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act References [ edit ] ^ Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 1 ^ "Information, Privacy and Archives Division (IPA)" . Ontario Ministry of Government Services . Retrieved 14 December 2013 . ^ a b Cavoukian, Ann (May 2013). "The evolution of freedom of information in Ontario: From reactive to proactice disclosure" . Academic Matters . Ontario Confederation of University Faculty Associations . Retrieved 14 December 2013 . ^ Tom Mitchinson (February
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the legal obligations imposed by MFIPPA, PHIPA, and PIPEDA. The original text explicitly states that information considered Private Information under these acts is also considered Confidential Information. The changed text exempts the provider from adhering to these acts and allows the provider to unilaterally determine what constitutes Private Information. This is a contradiction because the provider is no longer bound by the legal definitions and requirements of MFIPPA, PHIPA, and PIPEDA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states the 'Purposes' of MFIPPA and Snippet 2 confirms that the Act 'legislates access to information held by public institutions in Ontario subject to specific requirements to safeguard the personal information of individuals.' By exempting the provider, the changed text allows the provider to disregard the legal safeguards for personal information established by these Acts, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider will agree to use their best efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information to any third party for an indefinite period of time from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information to any third party for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and limiting the duration to one year weakens the protection of confidential information, especially considering the original indefinite duration. This might be insufficient under privacy laws that require long-term or permanent data protection for certain sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information as long as it is needed.  Limiting confidentiality to one year contradicts the requirement to protect information for as long as necessary, particularly when dealing with sensitive personal data. The term 'commercially reasonable efforts' may also be deemed insufficient depending on the sensitivity of the data.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of confidential information by changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and limiting the duration to one year. This contradicts PIPEDA's requirement to protect personal information as long as it is needed. The law snippet 2 states: 'limiting use, disclosure and retention, accuracy, safeguards, openness, individual access, challenging compliance' which aligns with protecting personal information. Limiting confidentiality to one year conflicts with the necessity of protecting information for the required duration, especially sensitive personal data.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider shall immediately notify the Town of any security breach (including any internal unauthorized use or disclosure), investigate the security breach, and take measures to reme diate such breach at the Vendor s cost as directed by the Town.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider shall notify the Town of a security breach within 30 days of discovery, and remediation will be determined by the Provider's internal policies, at the Provider's discretion and cost.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification to 30 days and giving the Provider full discretion over remediation contradicts the requirement for immediate action and remediation 'as directed by the Town.' This violates data breach notification laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to report breaches of security safeguards involving personal information to the Privacy Commissioner and affected individuals as soon as feasible. A 30-day delay violates the 'as soon as feasible' requirement and the provider alone being in control of remediation is against the spirit of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant delay (30 days) in notifying the Town of a security breach, contradicting the original text's requirement for 'immediate' notification. The modification also shifts control over remediation to the Provider's internal policies, undermining the Town's directive authority. The Law Explanation highlights that PIPEDA requires reporting breaches 'as soon as feasible'. The changed text's 30-day delay and provider-controlled remediation directly contravene this requirement, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition as it involves time-sensitive obligations not aligning with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The Town retains the right to audit the Provider to ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Town may audit the Provider once annually, with 60 days' prior written notice, and only regarding compliance with this specific agreement, not general data security practices.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the audit frequency, requiring advance notice, and limiting the audit scope hinders the Town's ability to ensure comprehensive data protection, potentially violating privacy regulations.
perturbation.law_citation: PHIPA, 2004, S.O. 2004, c. 3, Schedule A, s. 12
perturbation.law_explanation: PHIPA requires custodians of personal health information to take steps to ensure the privacy and security of that information.  Restricting the Town's audit rights limits its ability to verify the Provider's compliance with these obligations, potentially violating PHIPA.

perturbation.justification: The original text grants the Town broad audit rights to ensure data protection, while the changed text restricts audit frequency, requires prior notice, and limits the audit's scope. The Law Explanation states this potentially violates PHIPA, which requires custodians of personal health information to take steps to ensure its privacy and security. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that PHIPA aims to protect personal health information and outlines information practices and security measures. By limiting the Town's ability to audit the Provider's data protection measures, the modified contract hinders the Town's ability to verify compliance with PHIPA, creating a contradiction by weakening the oversight required to ensure data security as mandated by law. Therefore, the change contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be in full force upon its execution by the Parties and shall be and effect inde finitely. For greater clarity and certainty, the obligations herein with respect to Confidential Information continue indefinitely and beyond the time limit of the specific engagement for whi ch the Town engaged the Provider .
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be in full force upon its execution by the Parties and shall be in effect for the duration of the Provider's engagement by the Town.  All obligations herein, including those pertaining to Confidential Information, shall terminate automatically upon the conclusion of the Provider's engagement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligations to the duration of the engagement contradicts the requirement to protect personal information indefinitely when required by privacy law. Confidentiality obligations may need to extend beyond the engagement to ensure that private data is properly handled.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA states that organizations shall retain personal information only as long as necessary for the fulfillment of those purposes. This implies that the confidentiality obligations may outlast the business engagement. By restricting the data protection term to the length of the agreement, the modified text allows for situations where personal data is not adequately safeguarded for its entire lifespan as per PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text limits confidentiality obligations to the duration of the provider's engagement, which contradicts PIPEDA's principle that personal information should be retained only as long as necessary. The law explanation states that this could lead to situations where data is not adequately safeguarded for its entire lifespan, which PIPEDA aims to prevent. Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates PIPEDA outlines fair information principles, including limiting retention. The original text ensures confidentiality indefinitely. The change directly contradicts the principle of retaining data as long as necessary and introduces a time limit, creating a legal contradiction.
